title: Founders at Work
subtitle: Stories of Startups' Early Days
author: Jessica Livingston
description: A collection of candid, in-depth interviews with the founders of legendary tech companies — Apple, PayPal, Flickr, Hotmail, BlackBerry, Blogger, Twitter, Stripe, and more — about what those early days actually felt like, the near-death moments, the mistakes, and the stubbornness that turned ideas into enduring companies.
keyIdeas: Find a Broken World, Then Fix It
Co-Founder Fit Matters More Than the Idea
keyTakeaways: Nearly every great company went through a near-death moment in its first two years.
Founders rarely start with the idea that made them famous — they pivot into it.
Co-founder conflict is a top cause of failure; shared values beat complementary skills.
Talk to users relentlessly — most successful founders say 'I just listened to users.'
Do unscalable things to get your first 100 users: hand-deliver, fly to meet them, write personal notes.
Raising money is rarely the bottleneck; building something people want is.
Persistence and stubbornness matter more than brilliance — the founders who quit lost.
Originality is overrated; the 'boring' insight that users actually want this is underrated.
The 'aha' moment usually comes from noticing something the world is broken at, not from whiteboarding.
Speed beats polish — most successful companies launched as embarrassingly small prototypes.
whoShouldRead: First-time founders trying to understand what the early days actually feel like
Operators and employees at startups who want to understand the founder mindset
Investors looking for pattern recognition across generations of successful founders
Students of tech history who want the human side of companies they use every day

relatedBooks.title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
